---
title: "Exercise 13: Bayesian Optimization"
subtitle: "[Optimization in Machine Learning](https://slds-lmu.github.io/website_optimization/)"
notebook-view: false
---

::: {.content-hidden when-format="pdf"}
::: {.hidden}
{{< include ../_quarto/latex-math.qmd >}}
:::
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Reproducibility

Produced with Python 3.10 and R 4.4.1. To reproduce, install the pinned package versions below.

::: {.panel-tabset}
### Python
```bash
pip install numpy==2.0.2 matplotlib==3.10.8 scipy==1.15.3 scikit-learn==1.7.2
```
### R
```r
install.packages("remotes")
remotes::install_version("ggplot2", "3.5.1")
remotes::install_version("DiceKriging", "1.6.1")
remotes::install_version("patchwork", "1.3.0")
```
:::
:::

INSERT_problem_1

::: {.content-visible when-profile="solution"}
**Solution.**

INSERT_solution_1_text
:::

INSERT_problem_2

::: {.content-visible when-profile="solution"}
**Solution.**

INSERT_solution_2_pseudo

::: {.callout-note}
**R vs. Python.** Both use a Gaussian-kernel GP surrogate with the same closed-form EI, but they draw the 4 initial points from different RNGs (R's `runif` vs numpy's), so the specific evaluations - and hence the exact GP posterior and its uncertainty bands - differ between the panels. The method and where BO converges are the same.
:::

::: {.panel-tabset}
### R
{{< embed sol_bo_R.ipynb#bo_loop echo=true >}}
### Python
{{< embed sol_bo_py.ipynb#bo_loop echo=true >}}
:::
:::
